title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several routine items and made key decisions including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, directing staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations at $100/day fines, and recommending construction service agreements for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
2025 Weston Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely be moved to 2028. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settlement issues, and Scoffield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction for pavement marking and manhole issues.

Staff reported on spring road activities including removing road limits on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations along a tributary near Weston Avenue for approximately one week due to flooding, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from East Nick. Two culverts failed on Jones Street requiring additional repairs. Equipment maintenance and seasonal changeovers were detailed including servicing plow trucks and mowing equipment.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, ongoing well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system. In April, the village put 37 million gallons into the water distribution system but treated nearly 54 million gallons of wastewater - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified homes discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers, particularly affecting the Kathleen lift station. The INI study with In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas.

discussions.item: 2025 Weston Water Utility PSC Annual Report
discussions.body: Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million with net operating income of $482,911, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately 6,100 customers including 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. He noted grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing remain underfunded, requiring subsidies from general tax dollars. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. This compares favorably to City of Wausau paying $28/cubic yard versus Weston's $17.11/cubic yard, saving approximately $130,000 across three projects this year. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly sump pumps connected to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines between $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion about the approximately $70,000+ in extra treatment costs from clear water infiltration, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100 per day. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot one of Radkey Avenue where water/sewer mains currently dead-end. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, when the owner would pay assessment for main extension. Committee added language specifying reconstruction would occur no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with this addition passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Steam Construction is the contractor. Weston's share for water and sanitary sewer work is estimated at $94,860. Water main relocation is needed due to storm sewer improvements. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik intersection roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz due to having full-time staff rather than a summer intern for the extra $3,000. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not exceeding $100/day
Approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction (Weston share $94,860)
Award Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services to MSA ($37,315)
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Hold special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to investigate permanent pump solutions for Weston Avenue tributary flooding
Norcon to begin Scoffield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant to the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Election of officers (Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary)
Work product transmittals, educational presentations, reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected Trustee Erling as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by a commissioner, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making their event the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room usage from the Holiday Inn Express for future reporting. A motion to approve $5,000 (same as last year) was made, seconded by Jackson, and passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing meeting the candidates including winner Anastasia. She noted the candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, and this was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all members present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jennifer explained the building meets all B2 district zoning requirements and setbacks. Police Chief Jeremy discussed challenges with a gas pipeline running under the parking lot, which required rotating the building orientation to the east and utilizing the higher elevation of the site. The building is designed to accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond current staffing of 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed that may be built later depending on bid costs, with commissioners suggesting it be placed further north to accommodate future building expansion. Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Michael explained the village's wells are dispersed across four distinct areas making them more susceptible to contamination. Motion to approve the program with staff reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She will send email notifications and calendar invites to members.

Electric vehicle charging stations were noted for a future agenda, likely in the fall. Discussion arose about potential new TIF districts for residential/workforce housing. Jennifer mentioned Ehlers has a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, asking that staff waive excessive landscaping standards similar to accommodations made for the Greenheck Turner building to avoid unnecessary taxpayer expense. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure accountability and prevent the issue from being 'lost in the sauce.'
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-yearly inspections
Staff to report inspection program findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not completed wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations added to future agenda for fall discussion

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes and received overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and programs. No major policy votes were taken beyond routine approval of minutes.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg participating remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute (no items this meeting)
Educational presentations - Review of standing committee ordinances and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried with all in favor by voice vote.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under Section 2.04 subsection 5, covering Health Department, Social Services, and Veteran Services as direct departments, plus North Central Health Care and Aging Disability Resource Center as multi-county entities. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on strategic plan development through December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission of preventing disease and protecting public health. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen, the nurse family partnership program with first graduates, public health vending machines with free resources including tick kits, and a new FTE position for homeless system response created from a task force last session. The department has 37.6 FTE and approximately $5 million budget with about half from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard provided overview in Director Christa's absence, noting 140 FTE staff and $22 million budget with about $7 million from tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing root causes of housing instability and mentioned upcoming requests for unit restructuring due to increased needs.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the office increased to 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025. He noted $44 million in annual benefits coming to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits, housing stability work with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans).

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services, with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program funding remaining flat while food costs increased 40-45% over four years. The organization received its first levy increase in 2024 after holding steady since 2011.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate full presentation before committee
Health Officer to follow up on public health vending machine locations
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Strategic plan development work to continue through December
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested agenda items for future meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed ordinances regulating mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public Hearing on Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
Approval of Minutes from Previous Meetings
Acknowledge Reports from Committees and Commissions with Chair Updates
Consent Agenda - License Renewals and Vouchers

discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and from $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Trustee Motti spoke in favor, noting the fees are justified as population growth increases park needs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional inspection program where food trucks inspected by any participating municipality (including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others) receive a sticker valid across all communities, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously.

The board discussed Ordinance 26-012 amending chapter 82 regarding bicycles and e-bikes. Board members noted the distinction between e-bikes and e-motos (which can go 52 mph). Motion by Zagami, second by Clark, approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. After discussion about removing specific requirements for health/safety justifications, Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications, leaving only requirements for 12-hour notice, camera use, and exclusion from closed sessions. The amendment passed with two nos from Trustee Hermling and Trustee Motti. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lope Sorrell, approved unanimously. Parks, HR/Finance, and one CLIPS position remain open.

The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street, contingent on satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Motion by Hermling, second by Clark, with amendment by Beck, approved unanimously.

The board discussed challenges with the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park after losing their previous site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about vehicles parking on grass. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots and remains financially self-sustaining through grants and vendor fees. The board acknowledged this is discussion only with no immediate solution, noting if problems persist, the market may need to be suspended until a suitable location is found.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5202 Street criticized the board for hiring outside engineering consultants at an estimated $108,000 rather than filling the vacant engineering technician position. He also criticized the administrator for lack of accountability, noted that Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, complained about lack of transparency with committee appointments, and expressed disappointment that no detailed Kennedy Park project information was provided in the packet despite trustees requesting it at a previous meeting.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor regional inspection program established via Ordinance 26-008
E-bike regulations updated via Ordinance 26-012
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove health/safety justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending occupancy permit
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Committee and commission applications to be included in board packets going forward
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be brought back to the board

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris following a state-declared disaster affecting the Ringle area. The board also heard emotional public testimony from residents affected by recent severe weather requesting additional county assistance.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice and roll call
Committee appointments discussion and chair remarks
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks emphasizing leadership responsibilities and the goals guiding committee appointments, including increasing diverse perspectives and ensuring supervisor development opportunities. He noted over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion to approve committee appointments by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. The appointments included both county board chair and administrator appointments in one motion.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who praised her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). The board voted by paper ballot to select two members. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but not unanimously.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather affecting Ringle and other communities. The board suspended rules to hear this item directly from the Solid Waste Management Board (passed unanimously). Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to address the board, requesting the county waive fees entirely and extend relief for six months. The state waived $13.10/ton, Ringle waived $2.33/ton, and the county proposed reducing fees to commercial rate of $36.94/ton. Multiple supervisors asked clarifying questions about costs, eligibility verification, and future policy. The resolution passed unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty urged the county to waive fees entirely, stating private businesses have donated tens of thousands of dollars in cleanup assistance and arguing Marathon County should contribute similarly. She requested a six-month extension. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, noting 44 homes affected and many still awaiting insurance adjusters, requesting relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved increasing out of home placement reserve
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services budget
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to explore partnership with Community Foundation for reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Supervisor Gao requested notification of future solid waste board meetings to attend

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado, rather than the broader fee waiver originally proposed by staff. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and staff conclusions, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make previously non-conforming uses conforming. No natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacts
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff's recommendation to waive county zoning and septic system fees for properties damaged by the tornado. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to property owners affected by isolated incidents not declared disasters. Marilyn Bend asked about mound system replacement costs. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Sefeld noted the committee had just approved a fee increase during budget time. Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the proposal to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, excluding all other fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried. The amended resolution then passed but was noted as not unanimous, forwarding to the county board.

Director David Hagenbusher provided an overview of the department's operations, including the Bluebird Ridge landfill, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges requiring $6 million in capital projects, and response to the April tornado. He noted the department operates as an enterprise fund with 11 FTEs processing about 1,000 tons daily.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees who operate both county and city park systems. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems on properties damaged by April 17, 2026 tornado
Forward Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential) to county board for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) to county board for approval
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan to update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on dog days at the aquatic center to next month due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and Yellow Banks disc golf course maintenance. Two public commenters expressed concerns about dogs at the pool and questioned Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. Motion carried with no opposition on voice vote.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan development from 2023 to present, including the May 9th ribbon cutting. Director Sean Osterbrink explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for field lights with approximately $85,000 already at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal. Committee member Scott noted he wasn't on the committee when the project started and asked clarifying questions about timelines and village capital dollars. Staff confirmed no village capital funds are currently earmarked for Kennedy Park pending fundraising outcomes. The $8,700 referenced in public comment was explained as a Youth Baseball contribution for field equipment.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing locations of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during logging operations. Dan explained the mats are now village property with extras available for other projects. ATC/TC Energy representatives were on-site during the work and conducted a walkthrough afterward.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: Discussion was deferred to next month because Chair Katrina had an unforeseen issue preventing attendance. A resident named Andy who attended specifically to oppose dogs at the pool had his objection noted and was invited to return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete due to limited equipment access on the trail. Pressure washing equipment cannot reach the area except in winter via snow bridges. Committee discussed options including anti-vandalism coatings and volunteer workdays. No formal action taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version 7 of proposed signage for river launches, explaining signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks launches in Weston. Signs include QR codes for river gauge information and donation links to Marathon County Parks Foundation. Staff noted the Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously on voice vote.

Staff requested interest in discussing food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email request. Committee agreed to bring it back for future discussion along with the deferred dog day topic.

publicComment: Two public commenters spoke. Andy (6504 [street not fully audible]) opposed allowing dogs in the public pool, stating animals don't belong there and should use rivers and lakes instead. Jim Pensan (5002 Street, Weston) speaking via Zoom criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time, questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting as 'convoluted and misleading,' demanded to know where glow event funds and the $10,000 grant went, and accused Friends of Kennedy Park of lacking transparency.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at village launches - forwarded to village board
Staff to bring back food allergy awareness playground signage for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler for Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m.
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged financial reports including April budget status and TIF district reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam detailing clerk's office operations, election administration, licensing, and budget considerations. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentations - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

Committee discussed TIF district closure timeline. Staff indicated they are anticipating October-November for final bond payoff, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed final numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of clerk's office operations. The office has reduced from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time employee. She noted they handled the entire 2024 presidential election with just two staff members. Key topics included: election administration costs varying between even and odd years, Badger Book electronic poll book replacement at approximately $11,000 for four units, postage budget increasing from $6,000 to $15,000 for fall elections due to absentee ballot volume, and pet licensing being brought back in-house after 12 years with Pet Data saving approximately $55 annually. The quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' costs approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage to 5,089 postal patrons.

Clerk presented county survey data showing Weston's election inspectors are paid average ($12/hour) but chief inspectors are on the lower end. Pam indicated she will likely propose increasing chief inspector pay to $15/hour in the next election term due to their increased responsibilities.

Committee discussed recent increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 for neutered animals. Pam explained Marathon County contacted municipalities because they pay approximately $100,000 annually to the Humane Society but were only collecting about $65,000 from municipalities. The non-dog stray contract was also discussed, with committee members noting costs of $175-200 per animal including mice, suggesting future review of requiring law enforcement to bring in strays.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well with good vendor turnout and customer response. New food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft people have been added. Produce vendors will start coming mid to late June. Committee member reported vendors were very happy with the setup and location. Discussion included concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets during busy periods.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as this is his last meeting. He and his wife are moving away from Weston but staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Staff to continue seeking additional part-time clerk's office employee
Clerk to consider proposing chief inspector wage increase to $15/hour for next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both for further information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledge department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Unfinished business: Amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82 Traffic and Vehicles
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Code enforcement officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after a successful auction and sale, with the new owner submitting repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including the tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways) that were switched per last meeting's discussion. Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. The committee debated electric scooters used by children but decided to leave enforcement to officer discretion. Trustee Olsen motioned to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 4-5 hour windows overnight). Public Works Director Michael noted they rarely plow before 4-5 AM and suggested eliminating the regulation except for signing 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear cars before plowing. The committee deferred action, asking Michael to identify specific problem areas and bring recommendations to the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest school parking lot. Staff estimated $2,000-3,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for a lighted system. The committee was reluctant to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Trustee Olsen motioned that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost, the committee recommends board approval. Seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive about cost sharing.

Fire Chief explained a new regional inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston where one inspection is valid across all municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. The city of Wausau opted out to maintain their own program. Chwanka motioned to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing corrections: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), 3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence with apartment), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue with Weston Villa Apartments having nine separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to demolish an abandoned structure that has been posted since 2021 and abandoned since 2010-2014. A fire burn training exercise was previously rejected as the structure was too deteriorated. The current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer demolition; costs will be added to the tax roll. Olsen motioned to recommend authorization, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at Weston Municipal Center Park, predicting the grass will be destroyed by vehicles parking on it. He questioned whether parking on grass violates village code and suggested ADA handicap spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass for wheelchair accessibility. He questioned the origin of the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks. He also expressed concern about the age of some code enforcement cases, noting some have been on the list for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 Nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bicycle ordinance to Board with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails and public property
Public Works Director to identify specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions and return in July/August
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board for E911 compliance
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber for possible code violations

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
